_meta.philosophy: Curated, opinionated picks. 1-3 resources per topic. If a link is dead, replace it; do not add a fourth.

dsa.arrays.conceptSummary: Arrays/hashmaps are the most common interview pattern (~35% of problems). Master prefix sums, frequency counters, and the array-as-hashmap trick (using indices as keys for O(1) lookups).

resources.title: NeetCode 150 — Arrays & Hashing
resources.whyThisOne: Best-curated starting list. The 9 problems here cover the core patterns. Watch the videos when stuck.

resources.title: Sean Prashad's LeetCode Patterns
resources.whyThisOne: Patterns view, not problem-by-problem. Useful when you want to drill one specific pattern.

dsa.hashmaps.conceptSummary: Hashmaps trade space for time. Recognize them when the problem mentions 'find pair', 'count frequency', 'detect duplicate', or asks for O(1) lookup.

resources.title: NeetCode 150 — Arrays & Hashing
resources.whyThisOne: Same list as arrays — they're paired.

dsa.two_pointers.conceptSummary: Two pointers reduce nested loops to linear time. Use when the array is sorted, when looking for pairs/triplets, or when shrinking/expanding a window.

resources.title: NeetCode — Two Pointers playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Visual explanations. Watch one before solving the corresponding problem.

dsa.sliding_window.conceptSummary: Sliding window solves substring/subarray problems in O(n). Two flavors: fixed-size (easy) and variable-size (hard — track window state with a hashmap).

resources.title: Cracking the Pattern: Sliding Window
resources.whyThisOne: The clearest written guide. Memorize the template.

resources.title: NeetCode — Sliding Window playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Pair the article with these videos. Don't skip Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters.

dsa.stack.conceptSummary: Stacks handle nested structure: parentheses, expression evaluation, undo. Recognize them when the problem has 'matching', 'last seen', or 'undo' semantics.

resources.title: NeetCode — Stack playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Covers all the canonical stack problems.

dsa.monotonic_stack.conceptSummary: Monotonic stacks solve 'next greater/smaller element' problems in O(n). High-frequency in interviews and people whiff them. Drill until the template is automatic.

resources.title: Monotonic Stack — Aaron Kao guide
resources.whyThisOne: The clearest visual explanation of the monotonic stack pattern.

dsa.linked_lists.conceptSummary: Linked lists test pointer manipulation. Know the dummy node trick, slow/fast pointer (cycle detection), and reversal in-place. These three solve 80% of LL problems.

resources.title: NeetCode — Linked List playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Solid coverage. Reverse Linked List should become muscle memory.

dsa.binary_search.conceptSummary: Binary search isn't just for sorted arrays. The real skill is recognizing when a problem has a monotonic answer space ('what's the smallest X that satisfies...') and binary searching that.

resources.title: Powerful Ultimate Binary Search Template
resources.whyThisOne: The single best resource on binary search. Memorize the template; it covers all variants.

dsa.trees.conceptSummary: Trees are recursion's natural habitat. Master DFS (preorder, inorder, postorder) and BFS. Many tree problems become trivial once you pick the right traversal.

resources.title: NeetCode — Trees playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Comprehensive. Start with Invert Binary Tree, end with Serialize/Deserialize.

dsa.bfs_dfs.conceptSummary: BFS for shortest-path-in-unweighted-graphs and level-order. DFS for path existence, connectivity, and when you need to backtrack. Pick based on what the problem asks.

resources.title: William Fiset — Graph Theory series
resources.whyThisOne: The canonical free graph theory course. Episodes 4-6 cover BFS/DFS rigorously.

dsa.recursion.conceptSummary: Recursion is a tool, not a goal. Know base case, recursive case, and how the call stack unwinds. Practice converting recursive solutions to iterative when needed.

resources.title: NeetCode — Recursion fundamentals
resources.whyThisOne: Short, clean introduction. Pair with tree problems.

dsa.heaps.conceptSummary: Heaps solve 'top K' and 'running median' problems. Use a min-heap for top K largest (counterintuitive but correct) and max-heap for top K smallest.

resources.title: NeetCode — Heap/Priority Queue playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Covers the classic heap problems including K Closest Points and Find Median from Data Stream.

dsa.priority_queue.conceptSummary: Priority queues are heaps with semantics. Use them in Dijkstra, scheduling problems, and anything that needs 'process by importance'.

resources.title: NeetCode — Heap/Priority Queue playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Same as heaps.

dsa.intervals.conceptSummary: Interval problems (merge, insert, schedule) usually start with a sort by start time. After sorting, you're often looking for overlap conditions.

resources.title: NeetCode — Intervals playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Merge Intervals and Meeting Rooms II are classics. Drill them.

dsa.graphs.conceptSummary: Graphs are trees with cycles. Most graph problems reduce to BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, topological sort, or union find. Identify which one in the first 2 minutes.

resources.title: William Fiset — Graph Theory full course
resources.whyThisOne: If you watch one graph resource, this is it. Foundational.

resources.title: NeetCode — Graphs playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Interview-shaped graph problems. Pair with Fiset's theory course.

dsa.dijkstra.conceptSummary: Dijkstra finds shortest paths in weighted graphs with non-negative edges. Implementation: priority queue + visited set. Memorize the template.

resources.title: William Fiset — Dijkstra explained
resources.whyThisOne: Best visual walkthrough. Watch twice.

dsa.topological_sort.conceptSummary: Topological sort orders nodes in a DAG so dependencies come first. Use Kahn's algorithm (BFS with in-degree) for clarity. Recognize it when the problem mentions 'order' or 'prerequisite'.

resources.title: NeetCode — Course Schedule walkthrough
resources.whyThisOne: The canonical topological sort interview question, walked through clearly.

dsa.backtracking.conceptSummary: Backtracking explores solution space depth-first, undoing choices when they fail. Templates: combinations, permutations, subsets, n-queens. Master one and the rest fall.

resources.title: NeetCode — Backtracking playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Subsets and Combination Sum are the gateway problems.

dsa.tries.conceptSummary: Tries store strings character-by-character for prefix queries. Use them for autocomplete, word search, and 'find all words with prefix' problems.

resources.title: NeetCode — Implement Trie walkthrough
resources.whyThisOne: Once you can implement a trie from scratch, the variants are easy.

dsa.union_find.conceptSummary: Union find (disjoint set) solves connectivity problems in near-constant time. Use it for 'are these two connected', 'count connected components', and Kruskal's MST.

resources.title: Union Find — William Fiset
resources.whyThisOne: Best union find tutorial available. Watch the whole series.

dsa.dp_1d.conceptSummary: 1D DP problems have one varying dimension (usually array index). Climbing Stairs, House Robber, Coin Change. Pattern: define dp[i], find recurrence, identify base case.

resources.title: Errichto — DP for beginners
resources.whyThisOne: Most rigorous free DP intro. Will hurt; that's the point.

resources.title: NeetCode — 1D DP playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Interview-shaped 1D DP problems.

dsa.dp_2d.conceptSummary: 2D DP has two varying dimensions: usually two strings or a grid. Edit distance, longest common subsequence, unique paths. Draw the table.

resources.title: NeetCode — 2D DP playlist
resources.whyThisOne: Edit Distance and Longest Common Subsequence are the canonical 2D DP problems.

dsa.lis.conceptSummary: Longest Increasing Subsequence has two solutions: O(n²) DP and O(n log n) with binary search + patience sorting. Know both; the binary search version surprises people.

resources.title: LIS — both solutions explained
resources.whyThisOne: Covers both DP and binary search approaches.

dsa.coin_change.conceptSummary: Coin Change is the unbounded knapsack archetype. Two variants: minimum coins (1D DP) and number of ways (also 1D DP, different transition). Both interviewed.

resources.title: NeetCode — Coin Change
resources.whyThisOne: Walks through both variants.

dsa.edit_distance.conceptSummary: Edit distance is the prototype 2D DP problem. dp[i][j] = ops to convert s1[0:i] to s2[0:j]. Three transitions: insert, delete, replace.

resources.title: Edit Distance — NeetCode
resources.whyThisOne: Clearest walkthrough of the recurrence and table.

dsa.graphs_advanced.conceptSummary: Advanced graphs: Bellman-Ford (negative weights), Floyd-Warshall (all pairs), bidirectional BFS, A*. Most interviews don't go this deep, but staff-level loops can.

resources.title: William Fiset — Advanced Graph Theory
resources.whyThisOne: Continue the same series — episodes 15+ cover advanced topics.

dsa.shortest_paths.conceptSummary: Pick the right algorithm: BFS (unweighted), Dijkstra (non-negative weights), Bellman-Ford (negative weights), Floyd-Warshall (all-pairs). Match algorithm to problem in the first 2 minutes.

resources.title: Shortest Paths algorithms — Fiset
resources.whyThisOne: Comparison of Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford. Pick-the-right-tool framing.

dsa.mst.conceptSummary: Minimum spanning tree connects all nodes with minimum total edge weight. Two algorithms: Kruskal (union find) and Prim (priority queue). Rare in interviews but appears in design.

resources.title: Kruskal vs Prim — Fiset
resources.whyThisOne: Concise. Watch only if you have time after core graph topics.

dsa.mixed_review.conceptSummary: Mixed practice forces pattern recognition under pressure. Pull random mediums from LeetCode top-150, set 25-min timer, solve out loud.

resources.title: LeetCode — Top Interview 150
resources.whyThisOne: Random selection from this list = realistic mixed practice.

dsa.hard_problems.conceptSummary: Hards differ from mediums in scope, not novelty. Most are 'medium + edge case' or 'two mediums combined'. Don't panic; decompose.

resources.title: LeetCode — Hard problems with high acceptance
resources.whyThisOne: Filter by acceptance rate >40% — those are the 'approachable hards'.

sd.caching.conceptSummary: Caching trades memory for latency. Three write strategies (through/back/around) with different consistency tradeoffs. Cache invalidation is the hard part — there's a famous quote about that.

resources.title: ByteByteGo — Top Caching Strategies
resources.whyThisOne: Visual, concise, covers the strategies with tradeoffs.

resources.title: DDIA Chapter 1 (relevant sections)
resources.whyThisOne: If you own DDIA, the foundational caching sections are essential reading. The book is worth buying.

sd.caching_eviction.conceptSummary: Eviction policies decide what to drop when cache fills: LRU (recent), LFU (frequent), ARC (adaptive), FIFO. LRU wins most interviews; know when LFU is correct (skewed access patterns).

resources.title: Cache Eviction Policies Compared
resources.whyThisOne: Side-by-side comparison with use cases.

sd.load_balancing.conceptSummary: Load balancers distribute traffic. L4 = transport layer (fast, no payload inspection). L7 = application layer (slower, content-aware routing). Algorithms: round-robin, least-connections, consistent hash.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Load Balancing
resources.whyThisOne: Interview-focused. Covers what gets asked.

resources.title: Hussein Nasser — L4 vs L7 deep dive
resources.whyThisOne: The clearest L4/L7 explanation on the internet.

sd.cap.conceptSummary: CAP says: under network partition, choose consistency or availability. Real systems are PACELC (Partition→AC, Else→Latency/Consistency). Most interviewers want PACELC reasoning, not CAP buzzwords.

resources.title: Please stop calling databases CP or AP
resources.whyThisOne: Kleppmann (DDIA author) explains why CAP is misused. Read this once and you'll be ahead of 90% of candidates.

sd.databases.conceptSummary: SQL: ACID, structured, joins, vertical scale. NoSQL: BASE, flexible schema, horizontal scale, eventually consistent. Choose based on access pattern, not buzzwords.

resources.title: ByteByteGo — SQL vs NoSQL
resources.whyThisOne: 8-minute clear comparison.

resources.title: DDIA Chapter 2
resources.whyThisOne: Definitive treatment of data models.

sd.indexing.conceptSummary: Indexes trade write speed for read speed. B-trees for range queries (most SQL DBs). LSM trees for write-heavy workloads (Cassandra, RocksDB). Composite indexes have leftmost-prefix rules.

resources.title: Use The Index, Luke
resources.whyThisOne: Free book on SQL indexing. Read chapters 1-3 minimum.

sd.replication.conceptSummary: Replication = same data on multiple nodes. Leader-follower (simple, eventual lag). Multi-leader (complex, conflict resolution). Leaderless (Cassandra-style, quorum reads/writes).

resources.title: DDIA Chapter 5
resources.whyThisOne: The replication chapter. There is no better resource.

sd.partitioning.conceptSummary: Partitioning (sharding) splits data across nodes. Hash-based (even distribution, no range queries). Range-based (range queries work, hot spot risk). Consistent hashing (smooth resharding).

resources.title: DDIA Chapter 6
resources.whyThisOne: Partitioning chapter. Covers consistent hashing definitively.

resources.title: ByteByteGo — Consistent Hashing
resources.whyThisOne: Visual companion to the DDIA chapter.

sd.consistency.conceptSummary: Consistency models form a hierarchy: linearizable > sequential > causal > eventual. Strong = expensive. Most production systems use eventual or causal.

resources.title: Jepsen — Consistency Models
resources.whyThisOne: Authoritative reference. Bookmark it.

sd.idempotency.conceptSummary: Idempotent operations produce the same result if repeated. Critical for retry logic. Implementation: idempotency keys, deduplication windows, conditional writes.

resources.title: Stripe — Designing robust APIs (idempotency)
resources.whyThisOne: How a real production system handles idempotency keys.

sd.url_shortener.conceptSummary: URL shortener tests: hash generation strategy, DB choice, caching, read/write split, scale to billions of URLs. Surprisingly deep when pushed.

resources.title: Hello Interview — URL Shortener walkthrough
resources.whyThisOne: Interview-shaped walkthrough with tradeoff discussions.

resources.title: Alex Xu — System Design Interview Vol 1
resources.whyThisOne: Chapter 8 has the canonical URL shortener walkthrough. Worth buying the book.

sd.rate_limiter.conceptSummary: Rate limiter algorithms: token bucket (bursts allowed), leaky bucket (smooth), fixed window (simple, edge issues), sliding window (best). Distribute with Redis or per-host with sync.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Rate Limiter
resources.whyThisOne: All four algorithms, with distributed concerns.

resources.title: Alex Xu — Rate Limiter chapter
resources.whyThisOne: Vol 1 Chapter 4. Definitive.

sd.pubsub.conceptSummary: Pub/sub decouples producers from consumers. Kafka (durable log, replay). RabbitMQ (queue, push). Redis Streams (light). Choice depends on durability and replay needs.

resources.title: Kafka in 100 Seconds + deep dive
resources.whyThisOne: Fastest path to understanding Kafka's value prop.

resources.title: DDIA Chapter 11
resources.whyThisOne: Stream processing chapter. The deep treatment.

sd.retries.conceptSummary: Retries need exponential backoff with jitter (avoids thundering herd) and a circuit breaker (avoids cascading failures). Dead letter queues catch what can't be retried.

resources.title: AWS Architecture Blog — Exponential Backoff and Jitter
resources.whyThisOne: The canonical post on why jitter matters.

sd.backpressure.conceptSummary: Backpressure protects downstream systems from being overwhelmed. Implement via: bounded queues, reject-when-full, slow-down signals upstream. Without it, cascading failure.

resources.title: Backpressure Explained — Reactive Manifesto
resources.whyThisOne: Foundational definition. Pair with a real system example.

sd.notifications.conceptSummary: Notification system: multi-channel (push, email, SMS), per-user preferences, rate limiting, templating, delivery tracking. Fanout is the scaling challenge.

resources.title: Alex Xu — Notification System
resources.whyThisOne: Vol 1 Chapter 10. Complete walkthrough.

sd.task_queue.conceptSummary: Distributed task queue: producers enqueue, workers dequeue, ack on success, retry on failure. Consider: priorities, scheduled tasks, exactly-once semantics.

resources.title: How Celery works (architecture)
resources.whyThisOne: Real production task queue. Read the architecture sections.

sd.observability.conceptSummary: Three pillars: metrics (counts, gauges, histograms), logs (events), traces (request flow across services). All three needed; each answers different questions.

resources.title: Honeycomb — The Three Pillars of Observability
resources.whyThisOne: Practitioner perspective from a leading observability company.

sd.twitter_feed.conceptSummary: Twitter feed: fanout-on-write (push), fanout-on-read (pull), or hybrid (celebrities pull, normal users push). Hybrid is the production answer.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Twitter / X feed
resources.whyThisOne: Walks through all three approaches with tradeoffs.

sd.dropbox.conceptSummary: File sync: chunking, dedup, delta sync, conflict resolution, metadata vs content separation. The hard part is sync state machines, not storage.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Dropbox
resources.whyThisOne: Strong walkthrough with focus on chunking and sync.

sd.api_gateway.conceptSummary: API gateway: auth, rate limiting, routing, request/response transformation, observability. Single entry point for microservices. Don't put business logic here.

resources.title: Hello Interview — API Gateway
resources.whyThisOne: Interview-focused deep dive.

sd.ride_sharing.conceptSummary: Ride-sharing: real-time location tracking, driver-rider matching (geo-indexed), surge pricing, trip lifecycle, payments. Geohashing or quadtrees for spatial queries.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Uber
resources.whyThisOne: End-to-end ride-sharing design with geospatial focus.

ai.transformers.conceptSummary: Transformers replaced RNNs by using self-attention. Encoder-decoder (translation), encoder-only (BERT), decoder-only (GPT). Modern LLMs are decoder-only.

resources.title: The Illustrated Transformer
resources.whyThisOne: The single best transformer explainer ever written.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Transformers visually explained
resources.whyThisOne: Mathematical intuition without the math pain. Watch after the Alammar article.

ai.attention.conceptSummary: Self-attention computes how much each token should attend to every other token. Q, K, V projections. Multi-head = parallel attention with different learned projections.

resources.title: Attention Is All You Need (paper, readable sections)
resources.whyThisOne: Read sections 3.1-3.3. The original paper is more accessible than people think.

resources.title: Lilian Weng — Attention deep-dive
resources.whyThisOne: Comprehensive reference. Bookmark.

ai.tokenization.conceptSummary: Tokenizers split text into model-input units. BPE (most common), WordPiece, SentencePiece. Token count drives cost and context limits. Different tokenizers handle non-English languages very differently.

resources.title: Karpathy — Let's build the GPT Tokenizer
resources.whyThisOne: Karpathy builds a tokenizer from scratch. Long but unmatched depth.

ai.embeddings.conceptSummary: Embeddings map tokens (or sentences/documents) to dense vectors. Geometric properties: similar meaning → close vectors. Used for retrieval, clustering, classification.

resources.title: Cohere — What are Embeddings?
resources.whyThisOne: Practitioner explanation, less academic.

ai.serving.conceptSummary: LLM serving differs from typical web serving: long requests, GPU-bound, memory-constrained by KV cache, autoregressive. Latency = TTFT + per-token. Throughput vs latency tradeoff is everywhere.

resources.title: Lilian Weng — Large Transformer Model Inference Optimization
resources.whyThisOne: Reference doc for inference optimization. Read entirely.

ai.continuous_batching.conceptSummary: Continuous batching (vs static): inject new requests into a running batch as old ones finish. 10-20x throughput improvement. Foundational to vLLM and modern serving.

resources.title: Anyscale — How Continuous Batching Enables 23x Throughput
resources.whyThisOne: The canonical post. Required reading for AI infra interviews.

ai.kv_cache.conceptSummary: KV cache stores attention keys/values from previous tokens to avoid recomputation. Grows linearly with sequence length. Dominates VRAM at long contexts. PagedAttention (vLLM) manages it efficiently.

resources.title: vLLM — Efficient Memory Management with PagedAttention
resources.whyThisOne: Original PagedAttention post. KV cache management is interview gold.

ai.speculative_decoding.conceptSummary: Speculative decoding: small draft model generates candidate tokens, large model verifies in parallel. 2-3x speedup when draft is accurate. Tradeoff: extra compute on rejection.

resources.title: HuggingFace — Speculative Decoding explained
resources.whyThisOne: Practical explanation with code.

ai.vllm.conceptSummary: vLLM is the dominant open-source inference engine. Key innovations: PagedAttention (KV cache), continuous batching, optimized CUDA kernels. Know its architecture and tradeoffs vs TensorRT-LLM.

resources.title: vLLM — Architecture Overview
resources.whyThisOne: Official architecture docs. Pair with the PagedAttention post.

ai.inference_api.conceptSummary: Inference API design: request routing (model selection), batching, multi-tenancy, rate limiting, streaming, fallbacks. The 'design ChatGPT backend' question lives here.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Design an LLM Chat Service
resources.whyThisOne: Interview-shaped walkthrough.

ai.gpu_systems.conceptSummary: GPU systems for ML: VRAM (80GB H100, 192GB MI300X), HBM bandwidth (the actual bottleneck), tensor cores (matmul speed). Memory-bound vs compute-bound matters for optimization.

resources.title: Horace He — Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr
resources.whyThisOne: Best intro to GPU bottlenecks for ML. Memory-bound vs compute-bound framing.

ai.quantization.conceptSummary: Quantization reduces model precision (FP16 → INT8/INT4) for smaller memory + faster inference. Methods: GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant. Tradeoff: quality degradation, especially at INT4.

resources.title: HuggingFace — Quantization in depth
resources.whyThisOne: Practical reference covering all major methods.

ai.multitenancy.conceptSummary: Multi-tenant inference: noisy neighbor (one user blocking others), fair scheduling (token-level priorities), MIG (NVIDIA multi-instance GPU), per-tenant quotas. Interview gold.

resources.title: NVIDIA MIG documentation
resources.whyThisOne: How GPU partitioning actually works.

ai.cost_latency.conceptSummary: Cost/latency tradeoffs: model size vs quality (use smallest that meets bar), batching vs latency (bigger batches = more throughput, more latency), caching (KV cache reuse, response cache).

resources.title: Anthropic — Prompt Caching
resources.whyThisOne: Real-world example of caching as cost optimization.

ai.tensor_parallelism.conceptSummary: Tensor parallelism splits each layer across GPUs (each does part of the matmul). Communication-heavy. Used for models too large for one GPU. Pair with NVLink for bandwidth.

resources.title: HuggingFace — Model Parallelism Guide
resources.whyThisOne: All parallelism strategies in one place. Read tensor parallelism section.

ai.pipeline_parallelism.conceptSummary: Pipeline parallelism splits layers across GPUs (GPU 1 does layers 1-10, GPU 2 does 11-20). Lower communication than TP, but pipeline bubbles waste compute without microbatching.

resources.title: HuggingFace — Model Parallelism Guide (Pipeline section)
resources.whyThisOne: Same doc as TP — read both sections.

ai.model_sharding.conceptSummary: Model sharding strategies: TP (within layer), PP (between layers), DP (replicate model, split data), FSDP (shard model state). 3D parallelism = TP+PP+DP combined.

resources.title: PyTorch FSDP Tutorial
resources.whyThisOne: FSDP is the modern standard for training. Read the conceptual sections.

ai.eval_infra.conceptSummary: Eval infrastructure: how do you know your model regressed? Offline evals (benchmarks, golden sets), online evals (A/B, shadow traffic), human eval. Critical at Anthropic/OpenAI; underappreciated by candidates.

resources.title: Hamel Husain — Your AI Product Needs Evals
resources.whyThisOne: Practitioner perspective. Read the full post.

ai.model_gateway.conceptSummary: Model gateway routes requests to appropriate model based on task, cost, latency. Patterns: cascading (small model first, escalate on failure), routing (classifier picks model), ensemble (multiple models vote).

resources.title: Martian / RouteLLM (research overview)
resources.whyThisOne: Real implementation of LLM routing with cost/quality tradeoffs.

ai.rag.conceptSummary: RAG = retrieval-augmented generation. Pipeline: chunk → embed → store in vector DB → retrieve relevant chunks → inject into prompt. Quality depends on chunking strategy, retrieval method, reranking.

resources.title: Anthropic — Contextual Retrieval
resources.whyThisOne: State-of-the-art RAG technique. Required reading for AI interviews in 2026.

resources.title: Pinecone — RAG Handbook
resources.whyThisOne: Comprehensive RAG fundamentals.

ai.rag_platform.conceptSummary: RAG platform design: ingestion pipeline (parsing, chunking, embedding), vector DB choice, retrieval (hybrid: dense + sparse), reranking, freshness (re-indexing), eval. Many failure modes.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Design RAG System
resources.whyThisOne: Interview-shaped walkthrough.

ai.agents.conceptSummary: Agent orchestration: tool use, planning, multi-step reasoning, error recovery. Patterns: ReAct, plan-and-execute, multi-agent. Production challenge: cost (many calls), latency (sequential), debuggability.

resources.title: Anthropic — Building Effective Agents
resources.whyThisOne: Definitive guide to agent patterns from Anthropic.

ai.vector_db.conceptSummary: Vector DB indexes: HNSW (graph-based, fast, memory-heavy), IVF (clustering-based, lighter), PQ (compressed). Tradeoff: recall vs latency vs memory. HNSW dominates production.

resources.title: Pinecone — HNSW Explained
resources.whyThisOne: Best HNSW explainer. Pair with the IVF post on the same site.

ai.chatgpt_backend.conceptSummary: Design ChatGPT backend: streaming responses (SSE), conversation state, multi-model routing, rate limiting (per-user, per-org), abuse detection, content moderation. Combines AI infra + traditional SD.

resources.title: Hello Interview — Design an LLM Chat Service
resources.whyThisOne: Same as inference API — re-read with chat-specific lens.

behavioral.framework.conceptSummary: STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Be specific. Quantify when possible. Action should be 60% of the story — that's where signal lives.

resources.title: Jackson Gabbard — Behavioral Interviews
resources.whyThisOne: Ex-FB engineer, ran the bar raiser. Best free behavioral guide.

resources.title: Gergely Orosz — Behavioral Interview Prep
resources.whyThisOne: Senior+ perspective.

behavioral.ownership.conceptSummary: Ownership stories: time you saw something wasn't working and fixed it without being asked. Bonus: spanning beyond your team.

resources.title: Amazon Leadership Principles — Ownership
resources.whyThisOne: Even if not interviewing at Amazon, their LP framework is the bar for ownership stories.

behavioral.ambiguity.conceptSummary: Ambiguity stories: time the requirements were unclear and you had to define the problem. Show how you cut through, not how you complained.

resources.title: STAR method — same as framework
resources.whyThisOne: Re-read framework post with ambiguity lens.

behavioral.scaling.conceptSummary: Scaling stories: time you took something from working-but-fragile to robust-at-volume. Be specific about the scaling dimension (users, data, team).

resources.title: STAR framework
resources.whyThisOne: Same framework, different category.

behavioral.incident.conceptSummary: Incident stories: production broke, you handled it. Cover detection, mitigation, communication, post-mortem. The post-mortem and follow-through is what staff+ interviewers want.

resources.title: Google SRE Book — Postmortem Culture
resources.whyThisOne: Frames how senior engineers think about incidents.

behavioral.disagreement.conceptSummary: Disagreement stories: time you disagreed with someone (peer, manager, exec) and how you handled it. Bonus: time you were wrong and changed your mind.

resources.title: STAR framework
resources.whyThisOne: Use framework, focus on the action portion.

behavioral.mentoring.conceptSummary: Mentoring stories: someone you grew. Be specific about what they couldn't do before and could after. Senior+ roles need this.

resources.title: Rands in Repose — Managing Up
resources.whyThisOne: Browse Rands' archive for the engineering management lens.

behavioral.tech_leadership.conceptSummary: Tech leadership stories: time you drove a technical decision across a team or org. Show how you built consensus, not how you forced it.

resources.title: Will Larson — Staff Engineer
resources.whyThisOne: Free essays on staff+ engineering. Especially the archetypes and stories sections.

tools.mock_interviews.conceptSummary: Mock interviews are the highest-ROI prep activity. The gap between 'I can solve this alone' and 'I can solve this while watched and explaining' is huge and only closes through reps.

resources.title: Pramp
resources.whyThisOne: Free peer-to-peer mock interviews. Variable quality but free reps.

resources.title: Hello Interview — paid mocks with industry interviewers
resources.whyThisOne: Higher quality, real feedback. Worth it for the final 2-3 weeks.

resources.title: interviewing.io
resources.whyThisOne: Anonymous mocks with real engineers. Free first interview.

tools.coding_practice.conceptSummary: LeetCode is the standard. Premium ($35/mo) gets company-tagged questions, which is worth it for the final month.

resources.title: LeetCode
resources.whyThisOne: Standard coding practice platform.

resources.title: NeetCode
resources.whyThisOne: Curated lists + video solutions.
